author: @elizabethmckinney7182
text:
I would have liked to see more of the building process.  That is why I subscribe.  Also,  more info on "why" you're building a battery for a speaker.  

So,  how did you connect the batteries?

replies.author: @BASEDinMaine
replies.text: Probably in series, so that the voltage is additive since those cells are all probably less than 15v: connect + to - from one cell to the next.

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182
replies.text: @BASEDinMaine I meant physically connected. Did he spot weld them? Solder it? Tape? Rubber freakin bands? Lol... how are they connected??? These batteries had have very small contact points.... and lithium and great don't mix well.

author: @Teknopottu
text:
How did you test the capacity and internal resistance for safety? Claiming an old battery holds 3,5Ah and is safe to tape tightly together with other batterys is just silly. Even the good BMS's are not able to level the voltage every time if cells are of different values.

I use Liitokala Lii-500 for charging and testing my li-ions and ni-mhs. I've rigged alligator clips on it for testing other than cylinder shaped batterys.

 If internal resistance is too high (higher than 100mΩ I think it was) I mark the battery and test it separately for warming in charging and discharging. Many batterys hold good amount of charge but have worn and warm too much and get disgarded.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text: In one of the earlier video's Robert was testing the batteries for exactly the same reason as you cite.🙂
